title: People, Family & Belonging Overview
subtitle: Listen to the lesson explained in English

transcript.en: Hey! 👋 Unit 5. People — who they are, who they belong to, and the words that hold a family together.
Six sections: a **family chat**, **pronouns**, **this and that**, **family vocabulary**, **who owns what** with ta', and the brilliant shortcut of **attached pronoun suffixes**. Let's go!
The scene: someone asks about your family. Do you have a big one?
You say yes, I have two siblings — and notice the word used:
That's the phrase for 'two siblings'. Note that 'two' takes a special linking form before a vowel-starting word, and the word for siblings covers any mix of brothers and sisters. 🌟
Notice the suffix: 'my brother' and 'my sister' each carry the ownership right inside the word. Those little endings are Part 6's superpower. 💫
The verb there means 'he has'. Maltese says 'he has five years', just like in Part 2 of Unit 2. Consistent pattern! 👌
Seven pronouns. Most have a short form and a long form — both are fine. 'I':
See how the pronouns work in sentences. He works at the office:
Each pronoun pulls a different verb form. The verb changes to show who's doing the action — but you don't have to memorise it all at once. The exercises help you absorb it naturally. 🎯
Demonstratives — the words for 'this', 'that', 'these', 'those'. They change with gender and number, just like everything else in Maltese. 😄
Six little words — three for close, three for far. Memorise those six and you can point at anything in Malta! 🎯
The core family members. These are irregular in their plurals, so we give you both forms.
children — the shared plural for both son and daughter.
Family plurals don't follow a single pattern in Maltese — each one takes its own plural form. Memorise the pairs rather than guessing. 🧠
🏷️ PART 5 — POSSESSIVE: TA' AND THE PRONOUN SET 🏷️
Two ways to show who owns what. First and simplest: **ta'** — the word for 'of'. Noun + ta' + owner. Literally 'the X of Y'.
Note: the 'of' word contracts when it meets the definite article — you'll hear these contracted forms constantly. 🎧
Second way: **possessive pronouns** — mine, yours, his, hers, ours, yours (pl), theirs. They go AFTER the noun and agree with the OWNER, not the noun.
Same noun — il-kelb — same spot for the possessive: after the noun. Seven pronouns, one formula. 🌟
This is the superpower of Maltese. Instead of two words — 'my house' the long way — you can attach a tiny suffix straight to the noun and make it one word. Magic! 🪄
Seven small suffixes cover every owner: my, your, his, her, our, your plural, and their.
My house — the long way, then the short way:
Works the same for any noun. My father:
One word where English uses two. Once this clicks, your Maltese starts to sound very natural very fast. 🎯

Family dialogue. Seven pronouns. This and that. Family vocabulary with irregular plurals. Possessives with ta' and pronoun forms. And attached suffixes that collapse two words into one. That's Unit 5.
Lots here, but the exercises break it down one piece at a time. You're doing brilliantly! 💪
